title: Large $N$ factorization of families of tensor trace-invariants
authors: Sylvain Carrozza, Johann Chevrier, Luca Lionni
abstract: It was recently proven that, in contrast to their matrix analogues, the moments of a real Gaussian tensor of size N do not in general factorize over their connected components in the asymptotic large N limit. While the original proof of this rather surprising result was not constructive, explicit examples of non-factorizing moments, which are expectation values of trace-invariants, have since then been discovered. We explore further aspects of this problem, with a focus on Haar-distributed (or Gaussian) complex random tensors, which are more directly relevant to quantum information. We start out by exhibiting an explicit example of non-factorizing trace-invariant, thereby filling a gap in the recent literature. We then turn to the opposite question: that of finding interesting families of trace-invariants that do in fact factorize at large N. We establish three main theorems in this regard. The first one provides a sufficient combinatorial bound ensuring large N factorization, that is also simple enough to be applicable to various cases of practical relevance. Our second main result shows that the expectation value of any compatible trace-invariant is dominated by certain tree-like combinatorial structures at large N, which we refer to as tree-like dominant pairings. Our third main theorem establishes that any trace-invariant admitting tree-like dominant pairings does actually factorize at large N. In this way, we are able to prove that various families of trace-invariants that have been previously studied in the literature do factorize at large N. We apply our findings to the theory of multipartite quantum entanglement: to any trace-invariant is associated a multipartite generalization of Rényi entanglement entropy, whose typical expectation value in the uniform random quantum state can be explicitly computed assuming large N factorization.
summary: This paper investigates when the expectation values of tensor trace-invariants factorize in the large N limit. It identifies specific combinatorial conditions that ensure factorization and applies these results to compute the typical multipartite Rényi entanglement entropy for random quantum states.
main_problem: Investigating the conditions under which families of trace-invariants for complex random tensors exhibit large N factorization and determining the asymptotic behavior of multipartite entanglement measures.
main_result: The authors provide an explicit counterexample to factorization in the complex setting, establish new combinatorial bounds for factorization, and prove that compatible trace-invariants are dominated by tree-like structures at large N.
method: Combinatorial and graph-theoretic analysis of D-colored graphs, using Wick's theorem, asymptotic expansion, and the study of tree-like dominant pairings.
model_or_system: Complex Gaussian and Haar-distributed random tensors representing random D-partite quantum states in a C^N tensor product space.
key_observables: Trace-invariants, multipartite Rényi entanglement entropy, and moments/cumulants of random tensors.
important_parameters: Large N limit (asymptotic regime), number of partitions D, and the Gurau degree of the graphs.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the large N limit and focuses on Local Unitary (LU) invariant distributions; some results assume a power-law lower bound for trace-invariants.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a 3-colored graph and its associated trace-invariant; Figure 2 illustrates a modified graph with added edges and boundary vertices; Figure 3 shows a graph where components are connected by a single point; Figure 4 shows the recursive construction of a melonic graph.
paper_structure: The paper first addresses the lack of factorization in complex tensors with a counterexample, then introduces theorems for sufficient conditions for factorization via combinatorial bounds and tree-like structures, and concludes by applying these findings to compute multipartite Rényi entanglement entropy.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental tools for calculating entanglement properties in large-scale random quantum systems, which is crucial for understanding the entanglement structure of random quantum circuits and tensor networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
